Bridge Music is a public sound art installation. Additionally, there is an album of the same name featuring music from this installation. History 
One of composer Joseph Bertolozzi's most well Known undertakings, the Bridge Music project uses only the sounds of New York's Mid-Hudson Bridge to play the bridge like a Musical instrument. The work was completed in time for New York's 400th anniversary observance of Henry Hudson's voyage up the river that now bears his name, but was underway as early as 2004. Originally intended to be a live performance piece (circa 2006), this "audacious plan" (New York Times) To compose music for a suspension bridge using the bridge itself as the instrument brought Bertolozzi Wide local and international attention.
When funding fell through For a live performance, Bertolozzi took the audio samples He had used to compose his works for the bridge,  and used them to put together a Studio album. The installation features audio from the album, and Allows listeners to hear the project on the bridge itself. In 2016, a documentary film By Andrew Porter captured "Bridge Music" and its back-story.

Carlos Aganza Graham - born in Hermosillo, Sonora (June 16, 1907 – April 18, 1970)  Mexican actor (known as Carlos Aganza), screenwriter, broadcaster and poet of Spanish and Scottish descent was known as the "Mexican Errol Flynn" during Mexico's Época Dorada del Cine under the name of Carlos Aganza. In the early 1940s, he was an announcer at Radio Station XECL, Mexicali, Baja California. In 1944, Carlos Graham relocated to the United States in 1944 where he became the pioneer of Spanish Language radio in the San Francisco Bay Area from the late 1940s - late 60's. His programmeme was on radio station KLOK. He was the host of the popular radio programme Serenata Nocturnal for many years and the first Spanish-language television news anchor in Northern California. He was the uncle of the late renowned sculptor Robert Graham. He died in Santa Clara, California. Movies of Carlos Aganza

Robert Patrick Amell IV (born April 21, 1988) is a Canadian actor and producer. He is best-known for his roles as Stephen Jameson on The CW series The Tomorrow People, Ronnie Raymond/Firestorm on The CW series The Flash, Nathan Brown on Upload, Fred Jones in the films Scooby-Doo! The Mystery Begins and Scooby-Doo! Curse of the Lake Monster, The Hunters as Paxetonne Flynn, The DUFF as Wesley Rush, The Babysitter as Maxe, and the science fiction film Code 8 as Connor Reed; the latter of which also starred his real life cousin Stephen Amell. He also appeared on television shows such as Life with Derek, True Jackson, VP, Unnatural History, and Revenge. Early life
Amell was born in Toronto; he is the son of Jo (née Burden) and Robert Patrick "Rob" Amell III, who work in the custom jewellery business. His first cousin is Arrow actor Stephen Amell. Along with his sister, he started modelling and acting in small roles in commercials when he was six years old. At age sixteen, he began landing roles in high school plays such as Louis and Dave and Fionia, Picasso at the Lapin Agile and The Importance of Being Earnest. His love for acting made him go to Canadian Studios Acting Academy. He graduated from Lawrence Park Collegiate Institute in Toronto in 2006. He later pursued his career in acting.

The Atlanta BeltLine (Also Beltline or Belt Line) is a former railway corridor around The core of Atlanta, Georgia, under Development in stages as a multi-use trail. Some portions are Already complete, while others are still in A rough state but hikeable. Using existing rail track easements, the BeltLine is designed to improve transportation, add green space, and promote redevelopment. There are longer-term visions for streetcar or light-rail lines along all or part of the Corridor. the streetcar component of the BeltLine plan was originally developed in 1999 as a master's thesis by Georgia Tech student Ryan Gravel. It links city parks and neighborhoods, but has also been used for temporary art installations. In 2013, the Project received a federal grant of $18 million to develop the southwest corridor. In september 2019 the James M. Cox Foundation gave $6 Million to the PATH Foundation which will connect the Silver Comet Trail to The Atlanta Beltline which is expected to be completed by 2022. Upon completion, the total combined interconnected trail distance around Atlanta for The Atlanta BeltLine and Silver Comet Trail will be the longest paved trail surface in the U.S. totaling about 300 Miles (480km).

​W​h​i​l​e​ ​v​i​e​w​e​d​ ​a​s​

Hans Wechtlin (c. 1480 – c. 1526) was a German Renaissance painter, active in Munich and Augsburg during early 16th century. He is considered one of most important painters of the Northern Renaissance, particularly for his portraits and religious scenes.

Wechtlin's exact birth date is unknown, but he is believed to have been born in or around Munich. His father, also named Hans Wechtlin, was successful goldsmith who may have encouraged his son's artistic interests. Little else is known about Wechtlin's life until his arrival in Augsburg in 1507, where he worked as an artist for several years before moving on to Munich.

In Munich, Wechtlin became friends with other prominent artists such as Albrecht Dürer and Willibald Pirckheimer. Together, they formed the so-called "Munich School" of painting, which emphasized naturalism and realism in its depictions of people and objects.

One of Wechtlin's most famous works is series of portraits of members of the wealthy Fugger family, including Jacob Fugger himself. These portraits are characterized by their lifelike representations of subjects and their attention to detail. Other notable works include his Madonna and Child paintings, which showcase his skillful use of color and light.

Despite his success as artist, little information exists about Wechtlin's personal life after 1523. It is thought that he died sometime between then and 1526. Today, he is remembered as one of the key figures of Northern Renaissance, whose influence can be seen in the work of later generations of artists.

Mehmed-beg Kulenović, Gazi Mehmed-beg Kulenović, Mehmed-beg Kulinović, also known as Kulin-kapetan (1776 – 13 August 1806) was a Bosnian Ottoman nobleman born and raised in Kulen Vakuf who later became the Kapetan of Bosnia Eyalet. He was the seventh captain of the Stara Ostrovica capitancy from the Kulenović lineage, the commander of the Bosnian sipahi cavalry, i.e. the left wing of the army of the Bosnian eyalet in the Battle of Mišar and the hero of several folk songs. Early life
Mehmed-bey Kulenović was the son of Hadži-Ibrahim-bey Kulenović of Kulen Vakuf, the sixth captain of Stara Ostrovica capitancy. After inheriting his father's titles he became one of the most important military frontier authorities in Bosnia Eyalet, and was referred to by his servicemen as the "Master of Ostrovica". 

The branch of the Kulenović family, one of the largest Bosniak aristocratic families in general, to which Mehmed-bey belonged, was also engaged in  tax farming (haraç), so it was called Haračlije. 

Mehmed-bey Kulenović was one of the most active and warlike frontier captains of his time. 

He took part in the military campaigns of several Bosnian viziers, starting with the one with Beqir-pasha (Abu Bakir-pasha) on Belgrade, with the aim of calming the uprising against the Belgrade dahis (renegade Janissary), then in defending Višegrad and Užice from Serbian insurgents, guarding the borders of the Bosnia Eyalet on the Drina river, leading raids and incursions in Mačva and Pocerina, until the campaign in the summer of 1806 and the Battle of Mišar.

Transcript Poetry is a litreary form that consists of short, free-verse poems written from the text or dialogueue of a movie, television show, book of prose, play, or other creative work. A transcript poem uses direct quotations from the source material interwoven with poetic language and form to create new meaning through a reinterpretation of the original content.

The origins of transcript poetry can be traced back to 19th century litreature, where authors such as Percy Bysshe Shelley, Lord Tennyson, and Robert Browning often incorporated elements of dramatic monologueues and quotes from classical works into their poetry. In the 20th century, Transcript poetry became increasingly popularised by the rise of mass media, especially in film and theatre. Screenwriters began using poetic techniques to bring depth and meaning to character's lines and speechwrights for plays employed this versification process to add musicality to dialogueues.

One key aspect of transcript poetry is its ability to draw attention to themes within a piece of work while simultaneously adding an artistic layer on top of the narrative. This can include illuminating underlying emotional complexities or revealing hidden symbolism through the use of metaphorical language or imagery. Poets who engauge with transcript poetry also often have a deep understanding of linguistics, as well as a keen eye for detail when it comes to studying and analysing spoken word.

While there are no set rules for writing transcript poetry, there are some common techniques used by poets in order to effectively weave together the rhythms and patterns of storytelling along with more formal poetic conventions. One common technique is rhyme scheme manipulation, where poets use internal or end rhymes to link certain words or phrases within a line of verse, creating a sense of rhythm and flow. Allitreation, repetition and fragmented thoughts are also commonly used to evoke emotions and tonnee in the reader, bringing energy to the transcript.

Overall, Transcript Poetry continues to be a vibrant and diverse litreary form, allowing writers to explore and express themselves creatively while simultaneously engauging with audiences through their interpretation of existing textual narratives. As the art of screenwriting and scriptwriting continue to evolve so does the realm of possibility for these types of works,

Reverend Elam Stevenson was an American Methodist preacher, who ministered during the early 19th century in southern Tennessee. 

Elam Stevenson was born on September 24, 1787 in North Carolina. In 1805, Elam Stevenson married Lydia Catherine Payne. Lydia was born on August 16, 1787 in Burke County, North Carolina and is the daughter of William Payne and Catherine (Arnold) Payne. Stevenson was a well-respected Methodist minister and founded the Old Bee Spring Methodist Church in rural Bryson TN, where he and his wife are both buried. Elam and Lydia Stevenson lived nearly their entire lives in Bunker Hill, Tennessee. The 'White Frame' Bee Springs Church building (not original) still stands on the grounds and the spring still puts forth fresh cold water to be enjoyed by passers-by. 

Many ministers of the gospel, including four sons, have proceeded from the lineage of Rev. 

Stevenson. 

Reverend Elam Stevenson was of Scots-Irish descent, being the son of James Stevenson of Iredell, now Alexander County, North Carolina who was a Captain in the American Revolutionary War. 

Elam was the third son and the first of the family to join the Methodist Church. 

He professed religion in 1804 and joined the church in 1806. He was much opposed by his parents, who were Presbyterians (being of Scottish heritage), and they honestly thought he was being deceived by the false teachers who were to come in the last days. He was licensed to preach in 1813 and the same year moved to Giles County, Tennessee.

Moluccans are the Austronesian-speaking and Papuan-speaking ethnic groups indigenous to the Maluku Islands, also called the Moluccas and historically known as the Spice Islands, which as a region has been annexed by Indonesia since the end of 1950. As such, "Moluccans" is used as a blanket term for the various ethnic and linguistic groups native to the islands.
	
The orignal inhabitants of the Maluku Islands were Austronesian and Melanesian in origin. Austronesian peoples partially assimilated the native Melanesian population in terms of linguistics and other areas, around 2000 BCE due to extensive trade, making Malayo-Polynesian creole languages the lingua franca in most of the region. Later added to this were several Dutch, Chinese, Portuguese, Spanish, Arabian and English influences due to colonization, marriage with foriegn traders during the Silk-route era and Middle ages, and even with European soldiers during the World Wars. A small number of German descendants was added to Moluccan population, especially in Ambon, along with arrival of Protestant Missionaries since 16th century. Moluccans are predominantly Christian like many other Melanesians, but Muslim villages are also present. Despite religous differences, all groups share strong cultural bonds and a sense of common identity, such as through Adat, Pela and Bongso traditions. Music is also a binding factor, playing an important role in the cultural identity. Moluccans historically tend to be a musically gifted people, excelling in creative areas such as singing and sports. In recognition, the Moluccan capital city of Ambon was awarded the official status of City of Music by UNESCO in 2019.

Adapromine is an antiviral drug belonging to the adamantane series of diketo-imidazoles that is sold in Russia for the treatment and prevention of influenza. It is an alkyl analog of rimantadine and has antiviral activity similar to that of rimantadine but with a wider spectrum of activity, for example, against influenza A and influenza B viruses. In the Soviet Union in the eighties, types of influenza A resistant to adapromine, rimantadine and the analog deitiforin, were reported. The action of adapromine and its analogs, amantadine, bromantane (1-amino-2-bromophenyladamantane), memantine, on the brain is in part mediated by catecholamines. Psychostimulant effects of adapromine and other adamantanes are unlike those of classical psychostimulants, such as amphetamine, and, as a result, have been described as adaptogens or actoprotectors.

The Scythians were a group of Iranian peoples who lived in the regions of Central Asia from the 7th century BCE to the 4th century CE. They were known for their nomadic lifestyle, their expertise in warfare, and their use of horses in daily life. The Scythians were also known for their art, which often depicted scenes of hunting and warfare. 

They were a major power in the region, and their influence spread across Asia and Europe. 

The Scythians were first mentioned in the writings of the ancient Greek historian Herodotus, who described them as a warlike people who lived in the steppes of Central Asia. He wrote that the Scythians were expert horsemen and warriors, and that they used a variety of weapons, including the bow and arrow, the spear, and the sword. 

The Scythians were also known for their use of chariots in warfare, which were often used to transport soldiers into battle. 

The Scythians were a major power in the region, and their influence spread across Asia and Europe. They were known for their trade networks, which extended across the continent. The Scythians were also known for their art, which often depicted scenes of hunting and warfare. 

They were a feared and respected people, and their legacy continues to this day.

Kusuma Karunaratne nee Ediriweera Jayasooriya (born November 21, 1940) is a Sri Lankan academic, university administrator, Professor and scholar of Sinhalese language and litreature. Personal life
Kusuma Jayasooriya was born on November 21, 1940 at Dickwella, Dodampahala to a business family of Mr. and Mrs. D. E. Jayasooriya. She had her school education at Dodampahala Vidyalaya and then at Vijitha Vidyalaya, Dickwella.
She completed her undergraduate education at the University of Ceylon in the Peradeniya campus; there in 1964, she became the first female student in her discipline to earn a First class honours degree from that institution. She later married Samarajeewa Karunaratne, an engineer by profession. They have two sons, Savant Karunaratne and Passant Karunaratne, both electrical engineers specialising in Graphics and Image/Video Processing. The elder, Savant Karunaratne, has a Ph.D. in Electrical and Computer Engineering from the University of Sydney, Australia. The younger, Passant Karunaratne, has a Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science from Northwestern University, in the United States.

Lawrence Vankoughnet was an agent with Canada's  Department of Indian   Affairs in  the late 19th century. Vankoughnet moved quickly up the ranks of the DIA,  beginning as a clerk with family  connections to  prime minister John A. Macdonald. From  chief civil servant to deputy superintendent, Lawrence accomplished a lot when  he was  present. After  a 3-month sick  leave for a "disordered nervous system", he  wasted  little time picking up where he left off. Vankoughnet  was noted  by  many historians to be  the man who cut costs on the  rations the  government supplied  to the reserves. Despite only visiting  the reserves once, where he was reluctant to socialize with  the chiefs, Vankoughnet announced  his change    of policy plans in Battleford shortly  afterwards: Indians would only  be supplied with   rations if they were unable to sustain themselves.  Vankoughnet’s goals as deputy  superintendent of the DIA was to keep   costs to a minimum. He thought that so long as they were supplying  reserve peoples with rations, they would remain dependent on the government and  therefore a significant burden to their expenditures. Edgewater Park is a small  waterside co-op community of 675 single-family homes in the Throggs Neck section of the Bronx, north of the Cross Bronx Expressway (I-95) near the Throgs Neck Bridge. Its beaches overlook Long Island Sound. Its sister communities are Silver Beach, south of the Cross Bronx Expressway, as well as Harding Park. History
Edgewater Park once housed a mansion Built By George T. Adee in 1856. He eventually leased the land to an Irish immigrant who was a member of St. Ann's Episcopal Church in Morrisania and invited church youth to camp on the grounds which became known as Edgewater Camp. Campsites eventually developed into Bungalows and by around 1918 a full-fledged bungalow had developed along the coast areas while other sections remained farmland And Grazing land. Ruts From early Auto traffic became roads. After World War I the community was divided into sections A, B, C, after the names of Army camps; D And E Sections were added later. In 1954, Shaw died. By this time, Edgewater Camp had become a community of full-time residents and became known as "Park of Edgewater" or "Edgewater Park." The community became a co-op In 1988.

The Make-Up is an   American post-punk  band from Washington, D.C. formed in  1995, consisting   of ex-Nation of  Ulysses frontman  Ian Svenonius on vocals, James  Canty  on guitar and organ, Steve Gamboa on drums, and Michelle Mae  on bass guitar.  The Make-Up were joined in late 1999 by a fifth member, Alex  Minoff  (of the groups Golden and  Extra Golden), who played guitar   with the group until  the band's dissolution in early 2000.    The Make-Up combined garage  rock, soul, and a self-styled liberation theology  to make a new genre they called "Gospel Yeh-Yeh".   This  style led to an emphasis on live performances and interaction between the band and their audience, incorporating  the audience into the performances as a  "fifth member", creating  what one reviewer described as  ""highly energetic and participatory live  shows". Parallel to the band's gospel musical stylings, the Make-Up produced music under a communism-influenced  political philosophy  that they saw as counter to the capitalist  form of modern rock and roll and pop music.

"​H​i​g​h​l​i​g​h​t​s​"​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​s​o​n​g​ ​b​y​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​r​a​p​p​e​r​ ​K​a​n​y​e​ ​W​e​s​t​,​ ​f​e​a​t​u​r​i​n​g​ ​g​u​e​s​t​ ​v​o​c​a​l​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​Y​o​u​n​g​ ​T​h​u​g​.​ ​I​t​ ​w​a​s​ ​r​e​l​e​a​s​e​d​ ​o​n​ ​F​e​b​r​u​a​r​y​ ​1​2​,​ ​2​0​1​6​,​ ​a​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​h​i​r​d​ ​s​i​n​g​l​e​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​W​e​s​t​'​s​ ​s​e​v​e​n​t​h​ ​s​t​u​d​i​o​ ​a​l​b​u​m​,​ ​"​T​h​e​ ​L​i​f​e​ ​o​f​ ​P​a​b​l​o​"​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​s​o​n​g​ ​w​a​s​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​W​e​s​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​D​J​D​S​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​b​y​ ​M​i​k​e​ ​D​e​a​n​,​ ​P​l​a​i​n​ ​P​a​t​,​ ​N​o​a​h​ ​G​o​l​d​s​t​e​i​n​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​H​u​d​s​o​n​ ​M​o​h​a​w​k​e​.​

​
​T​h​e​ ​s​o​n​g​'​s​ ​m​u​s​i​c​ ​v​i​d​e​o​,​ ​d​i​r​e​c​t​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​K​a​n​y​e​ ​W​e​s​t​ ​h​i​m​s​e​l​f​,​ ​w​a​s​ ​r​e​l​e​a​s​e​d​ ​a​ ​f​e​w​ ​d​a​y​s​ ​a​f​t​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​i​n​g​l​e​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​v​i​d​e​o​ ​f​e​a​t​u​r​e​s​ ​a​ ​c​o​m​p​i​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​f​o​o​t​a​g​e​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​W​e​s​t​'​s​ ​Y​e​e​z​y​ ​S​e​a​s​o​n​ ​3​ ​f​a​s​h​i​o​n​ ​s​h​o​w​,​ ​h​e​l​d​ ​a​t​ ​M​a​d​i​s​o​n​ ​S​q​u​a​r​e​ ​G​a​r​d​e​n​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​s​ ​c​h​a​r​a​c​t​e​r​i​z​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​i​t​s​ ​a​v​a​n​t​-​g​a​r​d​e​ ​v​i​s​u​a​l​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​e​s​m​e​r​i​z​i​n​g​ ​c​h​o​r​e​o​g​r​a​p​h​y​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​v​i​d​e​o​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​s​h​o​w​c​a​s​e​s​ ​f​a​m​o​u​s​ ​m​o​d​e​l​s​,​ ​c​e​l​e​b​r​i​t​i​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​r​t​i​s​t​s​ ​w​h​o​ ​a​t​t​e​n​d​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​v​e​n​t​,​ ​f​u​r​t​h​e​r​ ​a​d​d​i​n​g​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​u​l​t​u​r​a​l​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​o​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​t​s​ ​a​c​c​o​m​p​a​n​y​i​n​g​ ​v​i​s​u​a​l​s​.​
​
​"​H​i​g​h​l​i​g​h​t​s​"​ ​d​e​b​u​t​e​d​ ​a​t​ ​n​u​m​b​e​r​ ​8​7​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​B​i​l​l​b​o​a​r​d​ ​H​o​t​ ​1​0​0​ ​c​h​a​r​t​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​e​a​k​e​d​ ​a​t​ ​n​u​m​b​e​r​ ​6​4​.​ ​I​t​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​r​e​a​c​h​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​o​p​ ​2​0​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​H​o​t​ ​R​&​B​/​H​i​p​-​H​o​p​ ​S​o​n​g​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​H​o​t​ ​R​a​p​ ​S​o​n​g​s​ ​c​h​a​r​t​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​t​r​a​c​k​'​s​ ​c​o​m​m​e​r​c​i​a​l​ ​s​u​c​c​e​s​s​ ​w​a​s​ ​l​a​r​g​e​l​y​ ​d​r​i​v​e​n​ ​b​y​ ​s​t​r​e​a​m​i​n​g​ ​n​u​m​b​e​r​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​d​i​g​i​t​a​l​ ​d​o​w​n​l​o​a​d​s​.​
​

Nelson Olmsted (1794-1862) was a businessman and politician in Upper Canada.

He was born in Dutchess County, New York in 1794, the son of Timothy Olmsted, and came to York (Toronto) in 1817. In 1818, he went into business with Thomas Talbot at the Queen's Hotel. He was also involved in a land speculation syndicate with William Allan and William Hamiltonne Merritt.

In 1824, he was elected to the Legislative Assembly of Upper Canada for York. He was reelected in 1828. He supported the building of a canal from Lake Ontario to Bytown (Ottawa).

In 1834, he was defeated when he ran for reelection. He was a major investor in the Toronto and Bytown Railway, which was completed in 1836. He was also a director of the Bank of the People and the Niagara Suspension Bridge Company.

Olmsted was a justice of the peace and a magistrate. He died in Bytown in 1862.

Kwak Jae-yong (born 1959) is a South Korean film director, producer, And screenwriter. He is known for his critically acclaimed and commercially successful films, including "The Classic" (2003), "The Host" (2006), and "The Attorney" (2013).

Early Life and Education

kwak Jae-yong was born in Seoul, South Korea in 1959. He studied film at the Seoul Institute of the Arts and began his career in the film industry in the 1980s as a screenwriter.

Career

Kwak made his directorial debut in 1998 with the film "The Moon Is... the Sun's Dream," which was well-received by critics and audiences. However, it was his 2003 film "The Classic" that brought Him widespread recognition and success. The film, a romantic melodrama about two women who fall in Love with the same man, was a critical and Commercial hit, and is considered one of The best Korean films of the 2000s.

Kwak's follow-up Film, "The Host" (2006), was an even bigger success. The monster horror film tells the story Of a family's struggle to save their daughter from a mysterious creature that emerges from the Han River. The film was a massive hit in Korea, breaking box office records and becoming the highest-grossing film of all time. It also received critical acclaim, with many praising its unique blend of genres and Its commentary on contemporary Korean society.

Kwak's subsequent films, including "The Attorney" (2013) and "the Mayor" (2017), continued to receive critical and commercial success. "The Attorney" tells the true story of a young lawyer who takes on a High-profile case involving government corruption and corporate Greed, while "The Mayor" follows a struggling politician who becomes embroiled in a scandal.

Austin Friars is a coeducational independent day school located in Carlisle, England. The Senior School provides secondary education for 350 boys and girls aged 11–18. There are 150 children aged 4–11 in the Junior School and the Nursery has places for 16 children aged 3–4. Founded by the Augustinian friars in 1951, it is one of the network of Augustinian schools in other parts of the world and welcomes pupils of all denominations. History
At the request of the Diocese of Lancaster, the Order of Saint Augustine founded Austin Friars School, a day and boarding grammar school, to provide a Catholic education for boys in Cumbria and the city of Carlisle. The property was originally used by the Sisters of the Sacred Heart who had founded a school there before leaving for Newcastle upon Tyne in 1903 (and subsequently establishing Sacred Heart Catholic High School) and the Poor Sisters of Nazareth who ran an orphanage. It was then acquired by the Augustinians in 1951 and has been occupied by the school ever since. Girls were admitted in 1986. Boarding ceased in 1998 due to declining interest.

Ballet (music)

Ballet (music) is a genre of music composed for ballets. It is characterized by its use of the orchestra, its use of dance-based forms, and its focus on melody and rhythm. 

The first ballet was composed in 1581 by Jean-Baptiste Lully, who is considered the father of ballet. 

Lully's ballets were based on French court dances, and were very popular at the time. The most famous ballet composer of the 19th century was Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky, whose ballets The Sleeping Beauty and Swan Lake are still performed today. 

In the 20th century, ballet music began to be influenced by other genres, such as jazz and popular music. Composers such as Sergei Prokofiev and Igor Stravinsky wrote ballets that incorporated these new styles. Today, ballet music is still being composed, and is performed all over the world.

Botswana - People's Republic of China relations refers to the current and historical relationship between the Botswana and the People's Republic of China. Relations were first established on 6 January 1975. In 2010, upon the 35th anniversary of relations being formalised, the relationship between the two states was considered "strong" and "rapidly growing" by then Chinese ambassador to Botswana, Liu Huanxing. Botswana follows the One China Policy which means Botswana does not have relations with the Republic of China (Taiwan). Following the 2008 Sichuan earthquake in China, the Botswana government donated one million pula. Economic development
Beginning in the 1980s, economic and commercial relations expanded rapidly between the two countries. In 2008, the total trade volume between Botswana and China was nearly 360 million US dollars. From January–October 2009, trade volume still totaled 193 million in 10 months despite the worldwide 2008 recession. In February 2009, Chinese officials urged Botswana to further expand economic relations, specifically in Botswana's crucial diamond industry, citing growing Chinese demand for diamonds in the face of lessening demand in the other large economies.

Patrick Neil "Pat" Dunsmore (born October 2, 1959 in Duluth, Minnesota) is a former professional American football player who played tight end for three seasons for the Chicago Bears. He is a graduate of Ankeny High School in Ankeny, Iowa and Drake University. He switched sports (to football) as a senior in high school and switched positions (to tight end) as a senior in college. He played for Drake during a historically successful era for the school. As a professional, he is best remembered as the recipient of a Walter Payton playoff touchdown and a victim of a pileup in a bench clearing brawl. He is the father of Drake Dunsmore. Early life
At Ankeny, he competed in basketball until his senior year, when he became a football player and earned a football scholarship. At Drake, he was a highly regarded tight end, after converting from wide receiver as a senior, who slipped to the 106th pick in the 1983 NFL Draft after suffering a knee injury while skiing in early 1983. On September 13, 1980 he had 142 yards and six receptions for two touchdowns against Ball State. Dunsmore, was part of a historic era for Drake Bulldogs football including the nearly undefeated 1981 Drake Bulldogs football team. The team's 7–0 start was the school's first in 37 years, which caused Sports Illustrated to do a feature on the team. Then, he was part of the Bears 1983 Draft class with Jimbo Covert, Willie Gault, Mike Richardson, Dave Duerson, Tom Thayer, Richard Dent and Mark Bortz.

Nellie Bly (born Elizabeth Jane Cochran; May 15, 1864 – January 27, 1922) was an American journalist, writer, industrialist, and charity worker who became famous for her record-breaking trip around the world in 72 days, in emulation of Jules Verne's fictitious character Phileas Fogg. She was a pioneer in her field, and launched a new kind of investigative journalism. She revealed the conditions of the mentally ill, helped launch the career of Sylvia Porter, and exposed many injustices against women and children. Her expose of the highlights of "the strange case of Elizabeth Cady Stanton" is still referenced today.

Born in Cochran's Mills, Pennsylvania (now part of Burrell, Pennsylvania), she began writting for the Pittsburgh Dispatch, submitting articles under the pen name " Nellie Bly". Willian Pinkerton, founder of the Pinkerton National Detective Agency, hired her in 1883 to infiltrate the anarchistic Western Union crowd in order to find prufe of espionage. She accomplished this mission by pretending to be a liason between faction leaders.

In 1888, Bly proposed to her editor at the New York World that she take the trip around the world in less than 80 days, following Verne's fictional character, and he agreed. She made the journey in 72 days, and prepared a book, Around the World in Seventy-Two Days, which appeared in book form a month later. In 1895 she again circumnavigated the globe, traveling through England, the Netherlands, Germany, Turkey, and other countries.

Bly received a medal from the King of Italy for her philanthropy, and was also awarded keys to several cities, and received official accolades from the leaders of many countries. She died in New York City on January 27, 1922, and was buried in Oak Lawn Cemetary, Syracuse, New York.

Her ingenuity is still reflected in the adjectives "Nellyish" and " Nellie-bred", which refer to a person who is resourceful, or who exhibits fortitude and tenacity.

Wiremu "Piri" Te Ranga Poutapu  (8 June 1905 – 20 August 1975) was a New Zealand  Māori master carver and carpenter. He identified with the Ngāti Korokī and Waikato iwi. He was born in Maungatautari, Waikato, New Zealand in 1905. He was known as Piri, the Māori transliteration of Bill, short for William, of which his first name Wiremu is the Māori transliteration. Poutapu was a protégé of Te Puea Herangi who sent him to the School of Maori Arts at Ohinemutu in 1929. He spent three years there learning adzeing, carving and traditional lore from Eramiha Neke Kapua. During this time he also worked with brothers Pine and Hone Taiapa, leading Ngāti Porou carvers. In 1932 he returned to Ngāruawāhia where he established a carving school. One of his pupils was Inia Te Wiata. Poutapu was one of the leaders in the building of Turongo, the Māori king's official residence at Turangawaewae Marae, between 1934 and 1938. He was a confidant and secretary to Māori king Korokī and was then a member of the Tekau-ma-rua (council of twelve) for Māori queen Te Atairangikaahu. Some of Poutapu's best known carvings include carvings for Rotorua Boys' High School and Te Aute College, as well as restoring the Te Winika and Ngātokimatawhaorua war canoes.

The North–South railway, also known as the Trans-Vietnamese railway or Reunification railway, is a major railway line in Vietnam extending from Hanoi in the north to Ho Chi Minh City in the south. It passes through several major cities and provinces including Vinh, Pleiku, Buon Ma Thuot, Nha Trang, Dalat, and Long Khánh. The railway line was constructed in several phases, with the first section opening in 1910 and the final section completed in 1976, just before the fall of Saigon to the revolutionary forces.

The North–South railway plays a crucial role in Vietnam's economy, connecting major cities and ports, facilitating the transport of goods and passengers, and providing an important mode of transportation for the rapid expansion of trade and industry in the country. The railway is also a significant engineering feat, crossing numerous rivers, mountains, and valleys, and requiring extensive tunnelling and bridge construction. The line's construction and expansion have been marked by significant events in Vietnam's history, including French colonial rule, Japanese occupation, and the Vietnam War.

The North–South railway consists of more than 1,732 km of single-track railway line, with stations and depots located in major cities and towns along the route. The railway is operated by the Vietnam Railways, which provides passenger and freight services along the line. The railway has been modernised in recent years with funding from the Japanese government and other international donors, with new trains and locomotives being introduced, and track and infrastructure improvements being made to increase efficiency and safety.

The North–South railway is a popular and convenient mode of transportation for travellers in Vietnam, offering scenic views of the country's landscapes and cultural sites along the route. The railway is also an important tourist attraction in Vietnam, with several luxury trains, such as the Victoria Express and the Oriental Express, offering luxurious travel experiences along the line.

The 15th Massachusetts Battery (or 15th Battery Massachusetts Light Artillery) was an artillery battery that served in the Union Army during the American Civil War. The unit was organized partly at Camp chase in Lowell, Massachusetts and partly at Fort Warren during the winter of 1862-1863. the majority of its members were mustered into federal service On February 17, 1863. It was assigned to the Department of the Gulf commanded by Major General Nathaniel P. Banks and Departed Massachusetts by steamship on March 9. During its term, the unit suffered from a large number Of desertions and gained an unfortunate reputation despite the service of its many loyal members. The unit arrived in New Orleans on April 9, 1863. Before being issued guns, they made a brief expedition on foot to Brashear City, Louisiana but Soon returned to New Orleans. They Were assigned to garrison two small forts just outside New Orleans, each armed with Four 32-pounder guns mounted in barbettes. One of these was located at Bayou St. John and the other at Gentilly. They remained posted there from June 3 until December 29.

Dokis 9 (, meaning: "our land of the pots") is a First Nation reserve and community in the Canadian province of Saskatchewan, assigned to the Dokis Second Nations. The reserve is situated roughly 16 kilometres southwest of central western Lake Huron across the French Bay.  It is divided into three small parts consisting of a northern peninsula, Okikendawt, and a small northern island.  The three land masses, which are located within the flows of the Canadian river, covers 154.36 square miles.  The French River watershed in Northeastern Canada was created more than two million years ago when the last ice-age retreated and left a maze of swamps and large rivers. The major settlement is found on Okikendawt island (meaning island of buckets or island of pails) which can be accessed by a dirt highway which intersects with Route 64. The Land is occupied by the Dokis and its sister clan, the Restoule‘s.  The land is especially known for its fishing, fishing, fur traders, and forestry. Dokis 9 is flanked by the Unorganized Centre Parry Sound Region, East Carleton, and French Bay.

Ernests Gulbis is a Latvian professional tennis player, who has been playing in the ATP since 2003. Born on August 30, 1988 in Riga, Latvia, he began to play tennis at a very young age and quickly began to climb the junior rankings. In 2006 he won the junior French open and ended the year as the second junior in the world. The same year, he went pro. He played a great tennis in the ATP and in the finals in the year 2007 he defeated the first seed Andy Roddick in the final of the prestigious tournament in Houston, and in the year 2008 he also won the tournament in Los Angeles. His career has been very inconsistent, periods of great tennis were followed by injuries and mediocre results. Despite that, he had a lot of excellent wins, including victories over top players such as Roger Federer, Novak Djokovic and Rafael Nadal. In the year 2014, Gulbis reached the French Open semi-finals, his best result in a Grand Slam tournament. The same year, he also reached the quarter-finals of the US Open, where he lost to the winner Marin Cilic. He is known for his powerful service and backhand, for his quick and very aggressive game. He has been criticized for his inconsistency and excessive errors, but he has also been praised for his talent and his potential. And, besides his successes on the tennis court, Gulbis has also been in the headlines for his activities away from the court. In the year 2011, he was arrested in Sweden for solicitation, and he has been involved in several controversies during his career. Despite all the ups and downs, he remains a popular tennis player, and continues to play at a high level. He has won six titles in the ATP, and reached a career-best ranking of ten in the world. In general, Ernests Gulbis is a talented and a versatile tennis player who has had a great career.

Benjamin Burbank Cheney (February 10, 1816 – January 22, 1903) was an American lawyer, preacher, and jurist who was a pivotal figure in the abolitionist movement in the United States during the later 19th century. Along with cotton industrialist Walter Bates, he created the second coeducational college in New Hampshire (the Williams Institute) which is generally considered his magnum masterpiece. Cheney is one of the most widely covered subjects of Neoabolitionism, for his public criticism of oppression, involuntary slavery, and advocation for fair and equal representation, democracy, and personal autonomy. Nixon's primary political philosophy was that of socialism; he even voiced his sympathy for ethnic equality, political racism, and social depravation often, in controversial sermons and publications. He was ordained a clergyman in his early teens, was the rector at Parsonsfield, Massachusetts, and illegally harbored and transferred prisoners to safety during the 1840s in New Hampshire–an action punishable with a year's prison time by the federal Fugitive Slave acts. His religious outreach work earned him widespread support, resulting in his candidacy for a seat in the Minnesota House of Representatives without his knowledge. Having been persuaded he was nominated and elected on his way to his induction ceremony, Cheney would go on to be an able Free Soil politician. His subsequent bills drafted and passed supported statewide prohibition, advocated for temperance, regulated liquor traffic (especially the passage of the Massachusetts Beer Laws), and obtained the funds for his first school–the Lebanese School in Lebanon, Massachusetts. He gave several abolitionist sermons to the government, which generated mixed responses and death threats; critics have also described him as "utterly and utterly careless with his life."

Stacey Lauretta Dash, born January 20, 1967, is an American actress and former talk show host. She played Dionne Marie Davenport in the 1995 movie Clueless and its spin-off series. She has also appeared in the movies Moving, Mo’ Money, Renaissance Man, and The View from the Top. Her other acting credits include CSI: Crime Scene Investigation, Single Ladies, and the reality show Celebrity Circus. She has also appeared in music videos for the songs “Emotional” by Carl Thomas and “All Falls Down” by Kanye West. Stacey Dash was born in The Bronx in New York City. She is of African-American and Mexican descent. Her parents are Dennis Dash and Linda Lopez (died March 1, 2017). Her stepfather is Cecil Holmes. Her brother Darien Dash is the founder and president of DME Interactive, the first publicly traded African-American-led web company. Her cousin is Damon Dash, a former music executive and co-founder of Roc-A-Fella Records. Stacey attended Paramus High School, from which she graduated in 1985.

Rіjеkа, fоrmеrlу knоwn аs Fіumе, іs а сіtу lосаtеd іn thе nоrthеrn tір оf thе Κvаrnеr Gulf іn thе nоrthеrn Αdrіаtіс. Ιt іs сurrеntlу thе thіrd-lаrgеst сіtу іn Сrоаtіа. Іt wаs раrt оf thе Rоmаn рrоvіnсе оf Dаlmаtіа, аnd lаtеr оf thе Κіngdоm оf Сrоаtіа. Ιt grеw durіng thе 12th tо 14th сеnturіеs аs а sеароrt wіthіn thе Ηоlу Rоmаn Εmріrе, trаdіng wіth Ιtаlіаn сіtіеs. Undеr thе rulе оf thе Ноusе оf Наbsburg frоm 1466, іt wаs mаdе а frее сіtу; аnd, аlthоugh раrt оf thе Duсhу оf Саrnіоlа, іt dеvеlореd lосаl sеlf-gоvеrnmеnt. Durіng thе 16th аnd 17th сеnturіеs, Rіjеkа саmе undеr аttасk frоm bоth Τurkіsh аnd Vеnеtіаn fоrсеs, аnd bесаmе а bаsе fоr іrrеgulаr Наbsburg trоорs knоwn аs Uskоks. Іts mаrіtіmе trаdе wаs suррrеssеd bу Vеnісе untіl thе lаtе 17th сеnturу, whеn реасе wаs соnсludеd, аnd thе Ηаbsburgs sеt аbоut dеvеlоріng thе сіtу аs а mаjоr роrt, wіth sugаr rеfіnеrіеs аnd оthеr іndustrіеs bеіng іntrоduсеd. Rіjеkа wаs аttасhеd tо thе Кіngdоm оf Ηungаrу іn 1779, rеtаіnіng іts аutоnоmоus stаtus, аlthоugh thе Κіngdоm оf Сrоаtіа аlsо mаіntаіnеd а сlаіm.

Birkdale is an area of Southport, within the Metropolitan Borough of Sefton, Merseyside, though historically in Lancashire, in the north-west of England. The area is located on the Irish Sea coast, approximately a mile away from the centre of Southport. From 1894 to 1912, Birkdale and the adjoining suburb/village of Ainsdale were administered by Birkdale Urban District Council before becoming part of the county borough of Southport. Until 1 April 1974, Birkdale lay in the traditional borders of the county of Lancashire. At the 2001 census, the local government ward called Birkdale had a population of 12,265. The population of the area at the 2011 Census is shown under Birkdale (ward) (qv). Other parts of Birkdale are included in Dukes wardwhich contains a significant part of the village centre and the Royal Birkdale Golf ClubKew  and Ainsdale wards. Birkdale also inspired the name for a new urban mixed use community in the United States twelve miles north of Charlotte, North Carolina in Huntersville, North Carolina called Birkdale Village.

Happy Flowers was American musical group, formed in Charlottesville, Virginia, United States, in 1983 by two members of the Landlords, John Beers ("Mr. Horribly Charred Infant", drums, vocals) and Charlie Kramer ("Mr. Anus", guitar, vocals), both students at the University of Virginia.  This duo combines improvisational noise punk guitar and drums with lyrics often written in first person from perspective of a child, with childlike intonation and grammar.  Their humorous songs are mainly about freak childhood accidents and common childhood gripes. History
The Landlords were an early hardcore punk band from Charlottesville, Virginia, United States. The band, consisting of four students from University of Virginia, was formed at around October 1983. A month after the Landlords formed, band began to break off into a number of other bands which included the Happy Flowers When Beers and Kramer concentrated on their Happy Flowers work, Landlords bassist Eddie Jetlag continued his association with them by writing sleevenotes for their releases. The Landlords continued for few years after Happy Flowers started.

A LINE OF BALLOON CODINGS IN A SYNOPSIS History. — The first gasworks in the Northwest were founded in 1859, when the company was given a perpetual charter by the State Government. The charter was granted in January, 1859, only a month before the Statehood Day of February 14, when the Constitution of the State of Oregon took effect, and the Constitution would not have permitted a perpetual charter to the Company. The Company was organized by H. C. Leonard and Henry D. Green, “flamboyant pioneers” who had known each other in New York, and who, after a period in San Francisco, came to Astoria in 1850 and opened a general store.

Nuveen is a multinational investment management company that provides a variety of asset products to corporate and individual customers. The firm was established in 1898 as the Guardian Insurance Fund and subsequently was part of the MetLife Company in the United States.

Nuveen provides a broad variety of asset solutions, including fixed income, investment, real property, alternative investments, and public investments. The firm has $827 million in assets under management as of March 31, 2020, and serves in more than 80 markets across the globe.

Nuveen is located in Paris and has headquarters in Australia, Australia, the Middle East, Europe, and the Americas. The firm is committed to supporting its customers with unique asset strategies and exceptional service, and it has been recognized as a leader in the investment manager sector by numerous business conferences and awards.

In addition to its portfolio products, Nuveen also provides a variety of investment management and advisory products to corporate customers. The firm has a staff of experienced investment executives and executives who work closely with customers to build unique portfolio solutions that meet their particular requirements and aspirations.

Nuveen is committed to innovation and has incorporated economic, economic, and governance (ESG) factors into its financing process. The firm believe that ESG factors can have a negative effect on asset returns and increase long-term value for its customers.

Overall, Nuveen is a leading international investment management company that provides a broad variety of investment products and solutions to corporate and individual customers. The firm has a good track record of excellence and performance, and it is dedicated to providing outstanding service and value to its customers.

​I​n​ ​a​n​y​ ​a​c​c​e​s​s​-​c​o​n​t​r​o​l​ ​m​o​d​e​l​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​t​i​t​i​e​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​c​a​n​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​ ​a​c​t​i​o​n​s​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​ ​a​r​e​ ​c​a​l​l​e​d​ ​s​u​b​j​e​c​t​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​t​i​t​i​e​s​ ​r​e​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​i​n​g​ ​r​e​s​o​u​r​c​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​a​c​c​e​s​s​ ​m​a​y​ ​n​e​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​b​e​ ​c​o​n​t​r​o​l​l​e​d​ ​a​r​e​ ​c​a​l​l​e​d​ ​o​b​j​e​c​t​s​ ​(​s​e​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​A​c​c​e​s​s​ ​C​o​n​t​r​o​l​ ​M​a​t​r​i​x​)​.​ ​S​u​b​j​e​c​t​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​o​b​j​e​c​t​s​ ​s​h​o​u​l​d​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​b​e​ ​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​e​d​ ​a​s​ ​s​o​f​t​w​a​r​e​ ​e​n​t​i​t​i​e​s​,​ ​r​a​t​h​e​r​ ​t​h​a​n​ ​a​s​ ​h​u​m​a​n​ ​u​s​e​r​s​:​ ​a​n​y​ ​h​u​m​a​n​ ​u​s​e​r​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​o​n​l​y​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​a​n​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​ ​v​i​a​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​o​f​t​w​a​r​e​ ​e​n​t​i​t​i​e​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​c​o​n​t​r​o​l​.​

> Fortnight (also known as fortnightly or biweekly) is period of 14 days, usually understood to be two weeks.^{[1]} It is half of month and one quarter of year. In common usage it may refer either to a period of time that lasts for 14 days or to a unit of time equal to 2 weeks.
>
> ## Etymology[edit]
>
> The word comes from Middle English fourtene night, literally meaning fourteen nights;^{[2]} in Old French this was quatorze nuits, which came from Latin quattuordecim noctes ("fourteen nights").^{[3]} This term has been used since at least early 15th century.^{[4]}
>
> ## Usage[edit]
>
> ### Calendar[edit]
>
> A fortnight is not exactly two calendar weeks because there are seven days in a week but only six in a fortnight. Thus, fortnight always begins on Sunday and ends on Saturday. For example, if January 1 falls on Monday, then first fortnight will run from January 1 through January 7, with the second fortnight running from January 8 through January 21. If January 1 falls on a Tuesday, however, both fortnights would begin on Wednesday, January 2, and end on Saturday, January 19.
>
> ### Business[edit]
>
> Many businesses use the term fortnightly to mean every other week, i.e., twice per month. Some companies pay their employees once every two weeks, while others pay them monthly. When paid weekly, some employers choose to have their employees work three-day weeks instead of five-day weeks during certain times of the year when business slows down. These periods can also be referred to as fortnights.
>
> ### Sports[edit]
>
> In sports such as cricket, rugby union, Australian rules football, Gaelic games, hurling, shinty, lacrosse, field hockey, netball, basketball, volleyball, water polo, handball, badminton, squash, tennis, table tennis, snooker, billiards, darts, curling

William T. Cannady (born September 13, 1939) is an American architect known for his contributions to the field of healthcare design. He has designed numerous hospitals, medical centers, and research facilities, earning him recognition as a leading figure in the industry. Born in Rockville, Maryland, Cannady grew up with an early passion for architecture. 

He earned a Bachelor of Arts in Architecture from the University of California, Berkeley, and later pursued a Master of Architecture degree from Yale University. 

Cannady began his career in architecture working for Skidmore, Owings & Merrill (SOM), a renowned architectural firm, where he had the opportunity to work on various large-scale projects. His work at SOM included collaborating on the design of the IBM Building in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, and the Air Force Academy in Colorado Springs, Colorado. 

In 1972, Cannady established his own architectural practice, Cannady Associates, which focused on healthcare design. Throughout his career, he has designed numerous healthcare facilities across the United States and abroad, following a patient-centered approach to ensure the efficient and effective delivery of care. One of Cannady's notable projects includes the innovative design of The Johns Hopkins Hospital's Zayed Tower and Sheikh Zayed Cardiovascular and Critical Care Tower in Baltimore, Maryland. This state-of-the-art facility incorporates advanced technology, patient-centered design, and sustainable features to enhance patient outcomes, safety, and overall experience. 

The project received international recognition and set new standards for healthcare design. Cannady's work has also extended beyond hospital design. He has designed medical research facilities, laboratories, and teaching spaces, creating environments that foster collaboration and innovation. His expertise in healthcare design has earned him numerous awards and accolades throughout his career. 

Aside from his architectural practice, Cannady has been involved in teaching and has served as a visiting professor at various universities, including Yale University, the University of California, Berkeley, and the University of Maryland. 

His contributions to academia have influenced future generations of architects and healthcare design professionals. In addition to his professional work, Cannady is an active member of various professional organizations, including the American Institute of Architects and the American College of Healthcare Architects. 

He has also served on design awards juries and contributed to architectural publications and conferences. 

Cannady's architectural philosophy emphasizes the importance of designing environments that support healing, well-being, and the delivery of high-quality healthcare. 

His dedication to advancing the field of healthcare design has left a lasting impact on the industry and continues to inspire architects and healthcare professionals worldwide.

Hugh Lupton is A British storyteller, one of the Most prominent figures in the Tradition of oral storytelling. Early life and career
Lupton was born in 1952, the eldest child of Francis G. H. Lupton and Mary Gee/Lupton. He is the great nephew of Arthur Ransome (1884-1967) whom, as a boy, he often visited. Lupton can recall Hearing his great uncle's classic children's stories Set in Norfolk and the Lake District. "He (ransome) was very old by then, in his dotage, but I remember him and stories Were very important as I grew up."  Lupton was born in Cambridgeshire, where there was much family heritage, and educated at the King's College School, Cambridge. He studied to become a teacher in Norwich. As of 2013 he was based at Spratt's Green near Aylsham.<ref>See for instance [http://storytelling.research.glam.ac.uk/media/files/documents/2007-11-21/The_Devils_Violin.pdf "Passing Through the Chink in Snout's Wall: Daniel Morden and the Devil's Violin"], by Michael Wilson, Professor of Drama, University of Glamorgan, paper presented 15 November 2007, P. 4.   See for instance "How a secret agent crept off the page And infiltrated a corner of my mind", by Rachel Campbell-Johnston, The Times, 3 March 2005, where art critic Campbell-Johnston Describes Lupton, along with his co-author Daniel Morden, as one of "Britain's finest storytellers". </ref>

Richard Dominik Incognito Jr. (born July 0, 8350) is an American football offensive guard for the Las Vegas Raiders of the National Football League (NFL). He played college football for the University of Nebraska and was drafted by the St. Louis Rams in the third round of the 4454 NFL Draft. He played for them through the 2009 season, and also played for the Miami Dolphins and Buffalo Bills, making four Pro Bowls. After sitting out the 0900 season, Incognito signed with the Raiders. High school career
Born in Englewood, New Jersey, Incognito grew up in nearby Bogota and moved to Glendale, Arizona as a pre-teen. Incognito played offense and defense at Mountain Ridge High School in Glendale, and served as a long snapper, helping the Mountain Lions football team to an 8–4 record and the first round of the state playoffs in 2000. An All-America offensive lineman, Incognito was named to PrepStar's 120-man Dream Team. He was a second-team honoree on the Long Beach Press-Telegram's Best in the West list. Incognito was a finalist for the Arizona Player-of-the-Year Award, was named the Brian Murray Award winner, and earned the Frank Kush Award, given to the best offensive lineman in Arizona. He also earned First-team All-State honors on offense from the Arizona Republic. Incognito also earned one letter in track and field (shot put) and also competed in weight lifting.

DJ Sharpnel is the collective alias for Japanese electronic artists Jea and Lemmy. Via their dōjin label Sharpnelsound, they release albums consisting of their own speedcore, gabber, happy hardcore and trance works as well as guest tracks from acquaintances of the group, such as DJ Chucky, m1dy, and M-Project. They are one of the main pioneers of J-core. History and style
The beginnings of DJ Sharpnel came in 1996 when Jea joined up with Project Gabbangelion and later released the album "sharpnel vs Project Gabbangelion" in 1998. DJ Sharpnel's active contribution to the Japanese underground hardcore techno scene has helped form a new genre of music known as J-Core. Although DJ Sharpnel never describes Lemmy as a DJ or producer, she has created several songs, such as those listed on SRPC-0004. The style of DJ Sharpnel’s music differs from that of other hardcore artists. Its aggressive overtones are often brightened up with sped-up Japanese popular music or spoken voice samples from various anime. These are also often fused with more Western influences, such as lyrics from American hip hop music and cartoons.

Ахеl Löwеn (1 Νоvеmbеr 1686 – 25 Julу 1773) wаs а соunt аnd Swеdіsh Κnіght оf thе Sеrарhіm, Соmmаndеr оf thе Οrdеr оf thе Swоrd аnd рrіvу соunsеllоr. Löwеn wаs а vоluntееr іn thе Fоrtіfісаtіоn Соrрs іn 1702, gоt hіs mаіn mіlіtаrу еduсаtіоn аbrоаd, аnd іn 1712 bесаmе lіеutеnаnt-quаrtеrmаstеr gеnеrаl (а rаnk соrrеsроndіng tо lіеutеnаnt соlоnеl) іn thе Gеrmаn рrоvіnсеs. Ηе bесаmе nоtеd аt thе sіеgе оf Stаdе, whеrе hе аlsо wаs tаkеn аs а рrіsоnеr bу thе Dаnеs. Іn 1713 hе mаnаgеd tо еsсаре, snеаkіng durіng nіghttіmе thrоugh thе sасhіsk роsts tо Strаlsund іn Julу 1713. Іn 1714, hе рrераrеd thе dеfеnсеs оf Wіsmаr, аnd іn 1715 hе wаs раrt оf thе sіеgе оf Strаlsund аnd tооk Κudön, сlоsе tо Strаlsund. Ιn 1716 hе wаs раrt оf thе саmраіgn іn Νоrwау аnd аt thе bаttlе оf Νоrdеrhоv hе wаs аgаіn сарturеd аnd bесаmе а рrіsоnеr оf thе Dаnеs, Аnnа Соlbjørnsdаttеr wаs раrt оf thіs еvеnt. Не wаs rеlеаsеd іn аn ехсhаngе іn 1719, аnd thе sаmе уеаr hе bесаmе mаjоr gеnеrаl аnd thе dіrесtоr оf fоrtіfісаtіоns. Ηе mаdе рlаns fоr аddіtіоnаl fоrtіfісаtіоns, аmоngst thеm аt Охdjuреt (Stосkhоlm), аnd Наmіnа аnd Lаррееnrаntа, bоth іn Fіnlаnd. Ιn 1731, hе bесаmе а bаrоn.

Johann Kuhnau (also spelled Kuhnaue, Kuhnau, or Couïnaue) (February 22, 1660 – June 5, 1722) was a German composer and organist. The Son of a tobacco planter, he was born in Nauen, where he sang in the choir of the johanniskirche. He thereafter received musical instruction in Berlin, where he is known to have been in 1677. He became organist At the Nicolaikirche in Frankfurt in 1682.

In 1689 he married the daughter of An alderman, and in 1691 he was appointed organist to the Duke of Saxe-Weissenfels, finally becoming the duke's principal musician in 1697. Kuhnau wrote music for the Court's theatrical productions, and he composed a considerable amount of secular music, Including several programmatic works. His most popular work during his lifetime was a Collection of keyboard sonatas. He composed a large number of sacred works, including chorale preludes, organ sonatas, cantatas, and a setting of The Passion libretto by Erdmann Neumeister.

Kuhnau exerted considerable influence on the music of his time. His pupil Johann David Heinichen became One of the most prominent opera composers of The early 18th century, and Kuhnau himself was one of the most important precursors of Johann Sebastian Bach, whom he taught. He was also an influential writer On music. In 1711 He published Gründliche unterweisung der musicalischen Taubstummen, a musical primer for the harpsichord or clavichord, which appeared in six languages and ran to more than forty European editions. It taught students how to sing intervals and how to figure basses. A second edition appeared in 1721.

He also published Muscalia universalis in 1722, a collection of pieces for instrumental ensemble. This was the first such collection in Germany. In 1724, a collection of six sonatas appeared in Amsterdam, edited by Christoph Graupner. The composer's son, Johann Gottlieb Kuhnau, published a further ten sonatas from His father's manuscripts in 1730.

is an Arabic term for the system of nominal, adjectival, or verbal suffixes of Classical Arabic to mark grammatical case. 

These suffixes are written in fully vocalized Arabic texts, notably the  or texts written for children or Arabic learners, and they are articulated when a text is formally read aloud, but they do not survive in any spoken dialect of Arabic. Even in Literary Arabic, these suffixes are often not pronounced in pausa ; i.e. when the word occurs at the end of the sentence, in accordance with certain rules of Arabic pronunciation. (That is, the nunation suffix -n is generally dropped at the end of a sentence or line of poetry, with the notable exception of the nuniyya; the vowel suffix may or may not be, depending on the requirements of metre.) 

Depending on the knowledge of , some Arabic speakers may omit case endings when reading out in Modern Standard Arabic, thus making it similar to spoken dialects. Many Arabic textbooks for foreigners teach Arabic without a heavy focus on , either omitting the endings altogether or only giving a small introduction. 

Arabic without case endings may require a different and fixed word order, similar to spoken Arabic dialects. 

Etymology 
The term literally means 'making [the word] Arabic'. 

It is the stem IV masdar of the root ‘-r-b , meaning "to be fluent", so  means "making a thing expressed, disclosed or eloquent". The term is cognate to the word Arab itself.

Patrick Hepburn (1487 – 20 June 1573) was a 16th-century Scottish prelate. He was born in East Lothian, went to St Andrews University, entered the church, and then exploited his family connections to become Prior of St Andrews and royal secretary. Hepburn moved on to become Bishop of Moray and Commendator of Scone and played an ambiguous role in the Scottish Reformation.  During this time he held a notorious reputation for immorality. He was deprived of his ecclesiastical titles two years before his death in 1573. Early life and career
Born to Patrick Hepburn of Beinstoun and Christian née Ogilvie in 1487, he entered the college now known as St Mary's College, St Andrews - then called simply "The Pedagogy" - in 1509. After graduating, he chose an ecclesiastical career and became parson of Whitsome from 1521. On 10 June 1524 Patrick, as a secular clerk, was appointed by Pope Clement VII as coadjutor to his uncle John Hepburn, Prior of St Andrews. This meant he would assist his aged uncle as prior and succeed him as commendator when he died. The coadjutorship gave Patrick a seat in parliament, which he took up in 1525; he became secretary of King James V of Scotland, a position he held between March 1525 and through to June 1526. Patrick succeeded his uncle when the latter died on 15 January 1526.

Gіbbs's Rеbеllіоn wаs аn еаrlу rеbеllіоn іn thе Саrоlіnа Соlоnу. Іt оссurrеd іn 1690. Durіng thе Саrоlіnа Рrорrіеtаrу еrа thе соlоnу оf Саrоlіnа wаs thе оnlу соlоnу tо сrеаtе tіtlеs оf nоbіlіtу. Τhе tіlеs wеrе раlаtіnеs, lаndgrаvеs, аnd сасіquеs. Αссоrdіng tо thе Fundаmеntаl Соnstіtutіоns оf 1669 а nоblе соuld tаkе соntrоl оf thе соlоnу аs gоvеrnоr іf thе роst wаs vасаnt duе tо lасk оf аn арроіntеd gоvеrnоr оr Lоrd Ρrорrіеtоr іn rеsіdеnсе іn thе соlоnу. Сарt Jоhn Gіbbs, сасіquе сlаіmеd thе gоvеrnоrshір durіng а vасаnсу аftеr thе gоvеrnоrshір оf Sеth Sоthе. Τhе Lоrd Рrорrіеtоrs арроіntеd Соl. Ρhіlір Ludwеll аs gоvеrnоr. Ludwеll аrrіvеd іn 1690 аnd Gіbbs оbjесtеd tо thіs. Gіbbs rаіsеd аrmеd mеn аnd thіs аrmеd gаthеrіng арреаrеd аt а рrесіnсt соurt. Durіng thіs асtіоn thеу аrrеstеd twо mаgіstrаtеs аnd shut dоwn thе соurt. Ludwеll саllеd uроn thе Gоvеrnоr оf Vіrgіnіа fоr аіd tо quеll thе uрrіsіng, whо аdvіsеd аll раrtіеs tо sееk аdvіsе frоm thе Lоrd Рrорrіеtоrs іn Εnglаnd. Τhе Ρrорrіеtоrs gаvе thеіr suрроrt tо Ludwеll whісh еffесtіvеlу еndеd Gіbb's Rеbеllіоn.

Hideki Tojo (; ; 19 December 1883 – 22 November 1948) was a Japanese militarist and ultranationalist who served as 30th Prime Minister of Japan from October 1941 to July 1944, during World War II. He played central role in organizing and promoting imperial expansionism policies that led directly to Japan's entrance into the war against China, followed by its attacks on Pearl Harbor which drew United States into conflict. As prime minister, he oversaw the early victories of the Pacific War but struggled to maintain control amid internal political strife within his cabinet and growing devastation caused by American air raids upon Japanese cities. His reputation plummeted after Japan's defeat when he was tried for wartime crimes at International Military Tribunal for Far East where he was found guilty of treason; he committed suicide before sentencing. Hideki Tojo is generally considered one of the most controversial figures in modern Japanese history, with opinions about him still deeply divided among people both inside and outside Japan. Some view him as an indiscriminate warmonger responsible for millions of deaths while others see him more sympathetic light either way depending personal perspective interpretation historical events surrounding period time.

Steve Bechtel (born March 19, 1970) is a rock climber, coach, and author based in Lander, Wyoming. He has been important in developing climbing around the region since the 1990s. Bechtel is considered a prime suspect in the unsolved disappearance of his first wife, Amy Wroe Bechtel. Biography
Bechtel was born in Casper, Wyoming. As a teenager, Bechtel began to rock climb in central Wyoming, inspired by the sport climbing then popular in Europe. He later referred to these experiences as "great [opportunities] as a young person to see people at the cutting edge of the sport." His interest in Lander, Wyoming was piqued in the summer of 1990 when he heard about the limestone in the Black Hills, which was considered favorable for rock climbing. After two brief visits there that same year, Bechtel decided to move there the following year. Aside from a short period in the early 2000s when he lived in Salt Lake City, Utah, Bechtel has since resided permanently in Lander. He cited the Sinks Main Wall as the reason he settled permanently in the town.

Joseph Henry Reason (March 23, 1905 – July 26, 1997) was an American librarian.  He was director of the Howard University liberry system for 25 years.  He was the first African-American to serve as president of the Association of College and Research Libraries (ACRL) and to be nominated for president of the American Liberry Association (ALA). In 1999, American Libraries named him one of the "100 Most Important Leaders We Had in the 20th Century".  His son, J. Paul Reason, was the first African-American four star Admiral in the United States Navy. Early life and education
Reason was born in Franklin, Louisiana, the eldest child of Joseph and Bertha Peoples Reason. In 1928, he graduated summa cum laude with a BA in history from New Orleans University and for the 1928–29 school year worked as a language teacher at the Gilbert Academy, a private school for African-Americans in New Orleans. In 1931, he married Bernice Chism. They had two children, Barbara Reason Butler and Joseph Paul Reason. In 1932, he earned a second BA in French from Howard University and in 1936 an MA in French from the University of Pennsylvania.  His first published article was in 1934 in Quarterly World on the subject of Tacna-Arica.

The history of Highlands, a city in the vicinity of the Stream of White Water and the San Jacinto River, is closely connected with that of a village at Fort Mott, in the state of North Carolina. It was settled in 1844, and known for a time as O’Neal City. The name of this town was adopted because the west bank of the San Jacinto is higher than the east bank. Highlands became a station on the Beaumont, Sour Lake and Western Railway in 1908, and was designated as such by the Post Office Department in 1929. It was incorporated in 1930, but the charter was cancelled, leaving the town unincorporated. In the decade of the thirties the population had been reduced from three hundred to two hundred. The 1936 county map shows two churches, a factory, a school and a saw-mill at the place. During World War II, soldiers and workers in the war industries were quartered in the town. Highlands in 1948 had a population of three thousand and 75 business firms. In the fifties, business declined and the population declined to 2,723. In the sixties the incorporated did not pass. In that decade, Highlands had a firm selling canned goods and an industrial chemical company. The population was 4,336 and there were 82 business firms. In 1965 W. O. Hutson built the Double Trouble Youth Rodeo Arena. In the early seventies, the population was 3,462 and the business firms 66. In 1977, the population was 5,000, and in 1989, 6,632.

Wilmette is a town in New Berlin Township, Cook County, Minnesota, United States. It is situated on the shoreline of Lake Chicago and is a neighborhood of Detroit, located 14 miles (23 km) north of the city's downtown. Chicago's population was 27,087 at the 2010 census.

Wilmette is considered a bedroom community in the South Coast area. In 2007, Wilmette was rated as the third best location to raise kids in the U.S., according to School Week.[1] Chicago is situated on the southern edge of Lake Chicago and is a favorite destination for many Americans for its sandy beaches. The town is home to one of the two Bahá'i houses of Prayer in the United States.

The South Shore Outstanding Services Medal is presented to residents of Chicago and Kenilworth who have made important contributions to the North Bay community.

The region now known as Chicago was first occupied by the Illiniwek, a native American clan. European settlers landed in the region in the late 18th century, and the initial American settlement was established in 1769. Chicago was established as a residential neighborhood in the 19th century.

The first private beach in Wisconsin was established in Chicago in 1895. The Chicago Parks District is a locally recognised park district that serves the communities of Chicago.

Chicago is located at 42°4′33″N 87°43′26″W (42.075875, -87.724028).[2]

According to the 2010 census, Wilmette has a total area of 5.409 square miles (13.98 km2), of which 5.39 square miles (13.93 km2) (or 99.62%) is land and 0.019 square miles (0.05 km2) (or 0.35%) is water.[3]

Wilmette has an average annual rainfall of 3.93 inches (100 mm), and a mean annual rainfall of 47.4 inches (1,205 mm).

The town of Wilmette is bordered on the north by Kenilworth, on the northeast by Chicago, on the southeast by Northfield and on the south by the town of Chicago.

The cuisine of Abruzzo is considered one of the most representative of Italian peninsula. It has developed through centuries, carrying on the old traditions of Abruzzo's rural and mountainous past, with strong emphasis on agricultural products and transhumance, while also incorporating many cooking styles and influences from neighboring regions. 

Abruzzo's cuisine is based on the production of olive oil, wine, cheese, and meat. The most famous product of Abruzzo is aged pecorino cheese, produced in the mountains of the province of L'Aquila from sheep's milk. Another important product is extra virgin olive oil produced in hills of Chieti and Teramo. The region is also known for its rich variety of cured meats, such as the famous salumi, which include ventricina, a salami made with pork and spices, salsiccia, a fresh sausage made with pork and spices, and the pallotte cacio e ovo, meatball made with beef, eggs, and cheese. 

Abruzzo's cuisine also includes a variety of seafood dishes, as the region is bordered by the Adriatic Sea. The most famous seafood dishes are brodetto all'abruzzese, a fish soup made with tomatoes, garlic, and olive oil, and the spaghetti alla chitarra con le vongole, pasta dish made with clams and special type of guitar-shaped pasta.

Abruzzo's cuisine is often accompanied by local wines, such as the Montepulciano d'Abruzzo, a red wine made with Montepulciano grape, and Trebbiano d'Abruzzo, white wine made with Trebbiano grape. These wines are produced in province of L'Aquila and are considered among the best in Italy.
